---
title: "Chapter 14: Disseminating Results and Achieving Policy Impact"
description: "Detailed study notes for Chapter 14 of Impact Evaluation in Practice."
book_id: "impact-evaluation-in-practice"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Chapter"
chapter_number: 14
source_pages: "277-287"
citation_key: "gertler_martinez_premand_rawlings_vermeersch_2016_impact_evaluation_in_practice"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:ch14-chapter-14-disseminating-results-and-achieving-policy-impact:c0001"
  - "chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:a-solid-evidence-base-for-policy:c0003"
  - "chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:tailoring-a-communication-strategy-to-different-audiences:c0003"
  - "chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:disseminating-results:c0001"
  - "chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:disseminating-results:c0004"
source_status: "needs_review"
---

## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2016</span><span>MCP evaluation-texts</span><span>Status: needs_review</span><span>Chapter 14</span><span>Source pages 277-287</span></div>

This note summarises Chapter 14, "Disseminating Results and Achieving Policy Impact," from Paul J.
Gertler, Sebastian Martinez, Patrick Premand, Laura B. Rawlings, and Christel M. J. Vermeersch's
*Impact Evaluation in Practice*
[@gertler_martinez_premand_rawlings_vermeersch_2016_impact_evaluation_in_practice]. It is a
paraphrased study note based on source-linked MCP research-library retrieval records, not a
substitute for the chapter.

The retrieval trail is retained in the source records at the bottom of the page. The note paraphrases the chapter's argument and procedures, and it does not reproduce textbook tables, checklists, figures, boxes, or extended passages.

## Core Argument

Policy impact depends on planning dissemination early, asking decision-relevant questions, and tailoring products and timing to the audiences who can use the evidence.

## Study Summary

Chapter 14 argues that dissemination begins before results are available. Evaluation questions and
stakeholder engagement shape whether the final evidence can answer live policy questions.

The chapter stresses audience tailoring. Technical reports, policy notes, briefs, presentations,
videos, blogs, and other products serve different users and should be planned with their decisions
and constraints in mind.

Negative or unexpected findings are not communication failures. They may be especially useful when
presented with enough context to support redesign, targeting, or theory revision.

A dissemination strategy should identify objectives, audiences, channels, timing, budget, and
responsibilities. The aim is not just publication, but credible evidence moving into policy
deliberation.

For close reading, track how the chapter uses dissemination strategy, policy impact, evaluation
question, stakeholder engagement while moving through policy relevance, audiences, communication
products. The notes identify the design problem the chapter helps solve and what should be checked
against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.

Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the implementation sequence.

## Key Concepts

- **dissemination strategy**: A planned approach to turning evaluation findings into products, channels, timing, and engagement for specific audiences.
- **policy impact**: The influence evaluation evidence has on program continuation, redesign, scale-up, termination, or wider learning.
- **evaluation question**: A policy-relevant, testable question that specifies the intervention, comparison, outcome, and decision need.
- **stakeholder engagement**: Deliberate involvement of policy makers, implementers, analysts, and other users in shaping questions, timing, interpretation, and use.
- **evidence use**: Application of evaluation findings to decisions about design, targeting, scale-up, continuation, reform, or termination.

## Chapter Structure

- Policy relevance.
- Audiences.
- Communication products.
- Negative findings.
- Dissemination planning.
- Use pathways.

## Practical Implications

- Plan dissemination while framing the evaluation questions.
- Produce different outputs for technical, policy, and public audiences.
- Treat unexpected results as opportunities for policy learning.

## Concept Map

::: {.concept-map}
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  Problem["Problem: What does Chapter 14 help decide?"]
  Central["Concept: Disseminating Results and Achieving Policy Impact"]
  Concept1["Concept: dissemination strategy"]
  Concept2["Concept: policy impact"]
  Concept3["Concept: evaluation question"]
  Mechanism1["Design element: policy relevance"]
  Mechanism2["Design element: audiences"]
  Mechanism3["Design element: communication products"]
  Implication1["Practice implication: Plan dissemination while framing the evaluation questions."]
  Implication2["Practice implication: Produce different outputs for technical, policy, and public audiences."]
  Question["Open question: What has to be checked in the source records?"]

  Problem --> Central
  Central --> Question
  Central --> Concept1
  Central --> Concept2
  Central --> Concept3
  Concept1 --> Mechanism1
  Concept2 --> Mechanism2
  Concept3 --> Mechanism3
  Mechanism1 --> Implication1
  Mechanism2 --> Implication2
  Implication1 --> Question

  classDef problem fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d;
  classDef concept fill:#e6f3f1,stroke:#115e59,color:#134e4a;
  classDef mechanism fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a;
  classDef implication fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#831843;
  class Problem problem;
  class Central concept;
  class Concept1,Concept2,Concept3 concept;
  class Mechanism1,Mechanism2,Mechanism3 mechanism;
  class Implication1,Implication2,Question implication;
```
:::

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Reading route: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Concepts synthesis: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)
- Previous: [Chapter 13: The Ethics and Science of Impact Evaluation](chapter-13-the-ethics-and-science-of-impact-evaluation.qmd)
- Next: [Chapter 15: Choosing a Sample](chapter-15-choosing-a-sample.qmd)
- Legacy extract retained: [What Is Impact Evaluation?](section-01-what-is-impact-evaluation.qmd)
- Legacy extract retained: [Constructing a Theory of Change](section-02-constructing-theory-of-change.qmd)
- Legacy extract retained: [Causal Inference and Counterfactuals](section-03-causal-inference-counterfactuals.qmd)

## Study Prompts

- What does Chapter 14 imply for selecting or defending an impact evaluation design?
- Which assumption in this chapter would be hardest to defend in a live policy setting?
- Which source record should be reopened before using this note in applied work?

## Source Records

- `chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:ch14-chapter-14-disseminating-results-and-achieving-policy-impact:c0001`
- `chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:a-solid-evidence-base-for-policy:c0003`
- `chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:tailoring-a-communication-strategy-to-different-audiences:c0003`
- `chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:disseminating-results:c0001`
- `chunk:impact-evaluation-in-practice:disseminating-results:c0004`

## References
